---
title: "Empirical Policy Simulation: Bridging Choice Science and Tobacco Regulation"
subtitle: "Candidate Presentation | UCSF Tobacco Center of Regulatory Science"
author: "Yichao Jin, PhD"
date: "2026-04-22"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    code-fold: true
    css: styles.css
---

<style>
.main-container {
    max-width: 1000px;
}
.hero-box {
    background: linear-gradient(135deg, #003262 0%, #005596 100%);
    color: white;
    padding: 2.5rem;
    border-radius: 15px;
    margin-bottom: 2rem;
    box-shadow: 0 4px 15px rgba(0,0,0,0.1);
}
.stat-card {
    border-left: 5px solid #005596;
    background: #f8f9fa;
    padding: 1.5rem;
    margin: 1rem 0;
    border-radius: 0 10px 10px 0;
}
.highlight {
    color: #005596;
    font-weight: bold;
}
</style>

<div class="hero-box">
  <h1>Bridging Discrete Choice Science & Regulation</h1>
  <p>Transforming Experimental Evidence into Predictive Policy Insights for Health Equity.</p>
</div>

## 🔬 Research Synergy: Why DCE Matters for TRS

Tobacco Regulatory Science (TRS) requires understanding how smokers and non-smokers will react to **hypothetical** policies (e.g., menthol bans, nicotine reduction). 

As a specialist in **Discrete Choice Experiments (DCE)**, I provide the "Ground Truth" for these scenarios:

*   **Quantifying Trade-offs:** Measuring how price, flavor, and health warnings interact.
*   **Predicting Substitution:** Anticipating shifts between combustible cigarettes and E-cigarettes.
*   **Targeting Vulnerable Groups:** Identifying disparate impacts on specific populations to promote **Health Equity**.

---

## 🛠️ My Technical Framework

I combine traditional econometric modeling with modern computational methods to ensure results are both **statistically rigorous** and **policy-relevant**.

::: {.stat-card}
### 1. Experimental Design (DCE)
*   Creating realistic choice sets based on FDA regulatory priorities.
*   Integrating product attributes that reflect the current market (Menthol, Nicotine levels, Synthetic nicotine).
:::

::: {.stat-card}
### 2. Population-Level Inference
*   Leveraging **PATH** and **NHIS** weights to ensure experimental results represent the US population.
*   Accounting for heterogeneity across age, race, and socioeconomic status.
:::

::: {.stat-card}
### 3. Predictive Simulation
*   Developing simulation tools to model "What-If" scenarios.
*   Providing regulators with an independent audit tool to verify industry claims.
:::

---

## 📈 Impact at UCSF

My goal is to integrate into the **UCSF Tobacco Center of Regulatory Science** to help answer critical questions:

1.  **Regulatory Policy Evaluation:** How will different subgroups switch products under new hypothetical regulations?
2.  **Market Shifts:** Predicting the substitution effects between combustible products and alternative nicotine delivery systems.
3.  **Communication Efficacy:** How do different public health warning labels impact choice behavior in real-time?

---

<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: 3rem; padding: 2rem; background: #eee; border-radius: 10px;">
  <h3>"Data-driven evidence is the strongest tool for public health."</h3>
  <p>Contact: yichao.jin@utdallas.edu | [yichao2022.github.io](https://yichao2022.github.io)</p>
</div>
